design_philosophy.core_loop: Strategic resource allocation via quarterly budgets → tactical hex-grid battles → territory control → political approval management. Player manages districts, commanders, troop deployment across the Thirteen Colonies while maintaining civilian/political support.
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Asymmetric win conditions: American side must survive and gradually accumulate strength against superior British forces; British must fortify existing positions and execute quick decisive strikes. Political approval acts as resource constraint—lose support and face removal as Commander-in-Chief.
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Lead a Revolutionary War faction (American Continental Army or British forces) through historically accurate strategic warfare. Make political and military decisions that shape the outcome of a historical conflict.
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Koei's uncompromising commitment to historical simulation complexity over accessibility. Game deliberately privileges historical accuracy and strategic depth over intuitive UI or spectacle. Explicitly researched detail in commanders, units, terrain, and political mechanics. Expects players to embrace 'acquired taste' complexity. Multiplayer and zero-player (AI vs AI) modes indicate design treats simulation itself as entertainment, not just player-facing systems.

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player agency is high but asymmetric: choice of faction (American vs British) and Commander-in-Chief (6 options) creates fundamentally different difficulty curves and strategic requirements. American side is explicitly harder (must survive longer). Game can end in defeat after 250+ hours of play, indicating no scaling assistance. Quarterly budget approval tied to political relationships creates dynamic difficulty through civilian approval mechanic—poor management can cripple military capability or force removal.

progression.upgrade_system: Unit recruitment, equipment purchasing, troop training within controlled districts. Militia (Patriot/Loyalist for Americans; German mercenaries for British) provide semi-autonomous resource gathering and can be recruited into regular command structures.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Territorial control unlocks district management and associated resources. Commander rank determines authority over autonomous units—only equal or higher rank allows militia subordination. Seasonal terrain changes provide dynamic unlock/lock of movement routes.
replayability_hooks: Six distinct Commander-in-Chief choices with different strategic profiles
Asymmetric faction play (American vs British require fundamentally different strategies)
Zero-player AI vs AI mode enables observation of emergent outcomes
Historical accuracy allows players to attempt recreating/diverging from actual Revolutionary War events
Procedural district autonomy via militia creates unique political/military situations per playthrough

world_building_techniques: Historical figure selection (Washington, Gage, Clinton, Howe, Ward, Lee) establishes narrative context
Quarterly political approval votes from Continental Congress/British Parliament simulate historical governance
District names and positions reflect actual Thirteen Colonies geography
Unit types (infantry, cavalry, guerrillas, engineers) and mercenary mechanics reflect historical Revolutionary War composition
Seasonal terrain changes embed time passage into map state
Militia autonomy mirrors historical civilian volunteer forces

faction_indicators: American vs British allegiance choice creates fundamental ideological split
Commander-in-Chief selection signals strategic preference (Washington = balanced, Gage/Clinton/Howe represent different British doctrines)
Patriot militia vs Loyalist militia divide reflects historical allegiances
danger_signals: Low political approval threatens removal as Commander-in-Chief—existential threat to playthrough
Loss of all troops in a district signals strategic collapse in that region
Poor budget allocation cascades into weakened military capability
Enemy commander recruitment/bribery indicates destabilization of controlled regions
environmental_storytelling: Fortified towns and forts represent strategic strongpoints requiring siege logic
Coastal access determines naval barrage capability and transport routes
Terrain impassability mirrors Revolutionary War geographic obstacles
District control map visualizes territorial power struggle across Thirteen Colonies
Seasonal terrain changes represent passage of historical time (1776-1783 timeframe)
